entry.title: GTS E4H PPF Condition is not moving up or down
entry.content:
Dear All,

We are using SAP GTS E4H Version. I have a created an Z Action definition . When I am adding this action definition in the Post Processing Framework (PPF) - Define Conditions/Output Parameters for Communication of General Documents , I am not able to change its position to up or down side . After selecting the arrow icons  for up and down it is not moving and getting the message below - Condition could not be moved .

Could you please help , how to  do this

Thanks 

Santosh

entry.title: Re: SAP CAP Lessons Learned: Reject error message missing on HTML5 Repository app?
entry.content:
Hi @hasanciftci, 

I’m facing the same issue.

When a user creates a record that violates a unique constraint, the CAP backend only returns 500 Internal Server Error in our cloud environment, so we can no longer identify the unique-constraint violation properly on the UI side and show a meaningful message.

Locally, the behavior is different, but in the deployed environment we only receive the generic 500 response. We started noticing this after upgrading from CAP 8 to CAP 9.

Did you find a solution or workaround for this in the meantime?

Best regards

entry.title: Integrating Joule with SAP Solutions
entry.content:
Thinking about SAP Joule and wondering where to start?

SAP has recently released a comprehensive integration guide that brings together everything you need to know to set up and integrate Joule across multiple SAP solutions. It walks you through the essentials, including the preview landscape, prerequisites, solution‑specific integration steps, document grounding, and more.

Whether you’re an administrator, architect, or consultant planning your next steps, this guide helps cut through the noise and get you moving faster.

If SAP Joule is on your 2026 roadmap, this is a great resource to bookmark as you begin your journey.

Integration Guide – Integrating Joule with SAP Solutions

Happy exploring—and as always, feel free to share your experiences or tips with the community!

entry.title: Re: Ledger Setup and its Role in Management Accounting in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
ICMR and Group Reporting covers the legal requirements while Group Valuation covers the Management perspective. While it is true that both approaches require the "elimination of intercompany profits", there are differences between these two consolidations.

One example: Your group is based in Europe and you own a subsidiary in India. However, due to local laws 51% is locally owned and 49% by you. Management / steering for the subsidiary is done entirely by the group, for that reason you will want to show the subsidiary 100% in management reporting. For legal reporting you must show 49%.

entry.title: SAP FICO wthholding annual accumulation limit configuration
entry.content: In my case, I am using the withholding tax section 194C in SAP FICO. According to section 194C, the tax rules are as follows: For instance, if it is a contract payment, a single bill above 30,000 directly incurs a 1% TDS. Similarly, annually, if the total exceeds 1 lakh, that is the limit. For example, if I have four bills each of 29,000, they do not cross 1 lakh initially, and also, each bill is below 30,000, so no TDS is applied. Now, if I make the fourth bill also 29,000, then the total annual amount will exceed 1 lakh. So, I need to deduct TDS there. My question is, how do I configure this accumulation—where do I set up the tracking of these cumulative billed amounts—so that when I raise the fourth bill, it flags that the annual limit is exceeded, and TDS must be applied?

entry.title: Mapping SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) Adapter to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ Adapter
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool.

Objective of this Blog

In this blog, we will focus on the mapping of SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter — with Transport Protocol “WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider” and Message Protocol both “JMS 1.x” or “JMS 2.0”) — to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter.

Since the Migration Tool skips the SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter, the approach described in this blog will help to manually configure both Sender and Receiver IBM MQ adapters in SAP Integration Suite iFlows based on source JMS (IBM MQ) SAP Process Orchestration scenarios.

Related Blogs 

  • IBM MQ JMS Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
  • Integrating IBM MQ On-Premise with SAP Cloud Integration Using the IBM MQ Adapter

SAP Process Orchestration JMS Adapter Modules

In SAP Process Orchestration, the following JMS Adapter Modules are commonly used to convert message payloads between binary and XML formats:                       

  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertJMSMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertBinaryToXMBMessage
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/SendBinarytoXIJMSService

In SAP Process Orchestration, these modules are often required when integrating with JMS providers such as IBM MQ. However, in SAP Integration Suite they are not required, as their functionality is provided directly by the IBM MQ adapter itself. The IBM MQ adapter in SAP Integration Suite offers built-in options for message conversion, allowing users to select either byte or text message formats directly in the adapter configuration.

Variants

Based on the SAP Process Orchestration source parameters, the migration will result in three different target variants:

- Sender

  • JMS Poll
  • JMS Subscribe

- Receiver:

  • JMS

Sender

The source Sender JMS MQ channel must have the following properties:

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Sender
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Sender variants — JMS Poll and JMS Subscribe — are detailed below (REST variant doesn’t apply).

JMS Poll

JMS Poll implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter actively polls messages from IBM MQ queues or topics at defined intervals, using a request-based approach to check for new messages periodically.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view not checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite

#

View

Parameter

View

Parameter

1

2

3

4

5

Processing

Message Destination Type = Topic → Topic Name/String

6

Connection

7

8

9

Processing

10

Source

Enable Topic Support checked → Client ID

Processing

Message Destination Type = Topic → Client ID

11

Source

Use SSL checked and WSMQ Cipher Spec = WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec → WSMQ Cipher Spec

Use SSL checked and WSMQ Cipher Spec = Other → Other Cipher Suite

Connection

SSL Cipher Suite

12

Processing

13

Processing

Processing

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Source view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “Other”:

Processing view

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

* After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

 JMS Subscribe

JMS Subscribe implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter establishes an active subscription to IBM MQ queues or topics, enabling real-time or near real-time message delivery as soon as messages are published to the destination.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

Same mapping as for the JMS Poll variant, but without fields 14, 15, and 16.

Receiver

The source Receiver JMS MQ channel must have the following properties: 

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Receiver
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Receiver variant — JMS — is detailed below (HTTPS variant doesn’t apply):

JMS

JMS Receiver implements a message publishing mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter enables SAP Integration Suite to send messages to IBM MQ queues or topics, acting as a message producer that publishes data to designated destinations in the IBM MQ server.

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Target view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “Other”:

Processing view

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

*After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

Advanced view

Conclusion

By following the mapping approach outlined in this blog — including detailed mapping tables and screenshots for the different target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ sender and receiver adapter variants — users can successfully migrate their adapters from SAP Process Orchestration scenarios to SAP Integration Suite iFlows.

 * Since not all parameters can be mapped, end users must evaluate whether any functionality configured in the source SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter has not been migrated to the target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter (e.g., PI Settings, Error-Handling Settings, or Duplicate Handling).

** In addition, some functionalities — such as correlation ID mapping — cannot be mapped directly but can be configured in the SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter. E.g., in the receiver adapter, it can be set dynamically using an exchange header or property.

entry.title: CDN Commerce API with the Composable Storefront OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE?
entry.content:
When putting a CDN in front of the API for SAP Commerce, the SAP Commerce Portal will have the "origin" of the API, but not the CDN's URL for the API. For example, the Portal may have "origin-api.example.com" configured to be hosted by the SAP servers, while the CDN points the public URL "api.example.com" at the origin-api.example.com.

When setting up the index.html files for the Composable Storefront, it is common practice to use <meta name="occ-backend-base-url" content="OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE"> which will replace the OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE with the first API endpoint from the portal for the environment that gets deployed to. This allows a single build from a single branch to be deployed to each environment (d1, s1, p1, etc).

However, that means that it will pick the origin-api.example.com non-CDN version of the API. So, how can the CDN URL be used? Does a special branch+build+deploy with a hard-coded occ-backend-base-url that explicity puts the CDN's api.example.com have to be used? Is that the only way?

entry.title: Re: Edit Existing Process Order Number Range Intervals or...
entry.content:
Dear @HO_IT,

In Public Cloud this requirement should be handled through configuration activity SSCUI 106461 Configure Number Range for Order Types. SAP Help states that this activity is used to define number ranges by determining internal number intervals for individual order types. In addition, SAP Note 3720445 confirms that when an order type is assigned a number range that has not been maintained the missing interval must be created in SSCUI 106461 Configure Number Range for Order Types in CBC. For process orders, SAP states that process order types are maintained in SSCUI 102801 Define Process Order Types and that every order type must be assigned a number range. SAP also states that when copying an existing process order type, you can either keep the existing number range assignment or choose a different one. Therefore the supported Public Cloud solution is to maintain the required interval in SSCUI 106461 and assign it to the relevant Process Order Types in SSCUI 102801. If the business requires different numbering logic for different scenarios the standard approach is to use separate process order types and assign different number ranges to each of them

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-manufacturing-execution/configuring-process-order-types

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3720445

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Enable Now goes WalkMe – Ein umfassender Rückblick auf die Roundtable Reihe 2026
entry.content:
Die dreiteilige Roundtable‑Reihe „SAP Enable Now goes WalkMe“ in Zürich, Berlin, Eschborn und im virtuellen Format markierte einen wichtigen Meilenstein für SAP Enable Now Kunden. Die Sessions boten einen sehr klaren und zugleich praxisnahen Blick auf die Transition von SAP Enable Now (SEN) nach WalkMe, die Positionierung von WalkMe Learning Arc und die Rolle von KI im modernen Enablement.

Die Diskussionen machten deutlich, dass die kommenden Jahre durch tiefgreifende Veränderungen in Lernprozessen, Content‑Erstellung und Performance Support geprägt sein werden. Viele Unternehmen stehen vor der Aufgabe, ihre bisherige Enable‑Now‑Landschaft strukturiert weiterzuführen, während sie gleichzeitig KI‑gestützte Ansätze, moderne Ausspielmechanismen und flexible Lernpfade integrieren.

Die Transition von SAP Enable Now zu WalkMe – Klarheit im Vorgehen, Chancen im Prozess

Fokus der Roundtables lag auf der Transition von SAP Enable Now zu WalkMe. Viele Teilnehmer:innen formulierten ein gemeinsames Anliegen: Planungssicherheit, Transparenz und ein Verständnis darüber, wie die Migration technisch, fachlich und organisatorisch gelingt.

SEN bleibt bis Ende 2030 in der Wartung. Für diese Zeitspanne können Inhalte weiterhin gepflegt und produziert werden. Danach stehen bestehende Lernobjekte weiterhin zur Verfügung, lassen sich jedoch nicht mehr editieren. Dadurch entsteht ein stabiler Rahmen, in dem Unternehmen die Transition bewusst gestalten und den Aufwand sinnvoll auf die einzelnen Projektphasen verteilen können. Besonders hilfreich: Wer als SEN-Bestandskunde WalkMe Learning Arc lizenziert, behält den Zugang zum SEN Producer, ohne zusätzliche Lizenzkosten!

Der technische Migrationspfad basiert auf einem API‑basierten Workspace‑Transfer. Dabei wird der bestehende SEN‑Workspace in eine Learning‑Arc‑Instanz kopiert. Die Migration erfolgt unter drei Gesichtspunkten: „hostable“, „playable“ und „editable. „Hostable“ beschreibt die Übernahme der SEN‑Inhalte in die WalkMe‑Infrastruktur. „Playable“ bedeutet, dass die alten SEN‑Inhalte auch nach der Migration lauffähig bleiben und in WalkMe Content-Strukturen eingebaut werden können. „Editable“ schließlich bezieht sich auf die Bearbeitung in WalkMe Learning Arc mit Hilfe des zur Verfügung stehenden SEN Producers.

 

Während der Übertragungsprozess zuverlässig funktioniert, überträgt er jedoch keine Versionshistorien, sondern lediglich den jeweils aktuellen Stand jedes Lernobjekts. Auch die Rollen‑ oder Segmentierungslogik muss nach dem Transfer neu erstellt werden, was auf der gleichen technologischen Grundlage erfolgt und daher keinen nennenswerten Aufwand erzeugt. Jedes Unternehmen sollte vorab definieren, welche Inhalte in welcher Struktur überführt werden sollen. Die Diskussionen der diesjährigen Roundtables zeigen, dass es sinnvoll ist, bestehende SEN‑Strukturen kritisch zu prüfen und strukturell zu entschlacken, bevor sie nach WalkMe überführt werden.

 

Die größte fachliche Abweichung betrifft den SEN Companion‑Content. In‑App‑Hilfen und Guided Tours werden nicht übertragen und müssen in WalkMe neu erstellt werden. Unternehmen, die bisher stark mit Companion gearbeitet haben, müssen daher gezielt entscheiden, wie sie den zukünftigen Performance Support gestalten. Klar ist, dass WalkMe als Marktführer im DAP Segment hier weit mehr Möglichkeiten bietet als bisher.

Um die Transition zeitlich zu entzerren, wurde die „Test‑&‑Go‑Live‑SKU“ vorgestellt. Sie ermöglicht für vier bis elf Monate einen Parallelbetrieb der beiden Systeme. Das verschafft Raum für einen kontrollierten Übergang, ohne Projekte unter Druck zu setzen, und wurde von vielen Teilnehmenden ausdrücklich als zentraler Erfolgsfaktor genannt.

WalkMe Learning Arc – Die neue Lernplattform im SAP‑Kontext

WalkMe Learning Arc wurde auf allen Veranstaltungen ausführlich demonstriert und traf auf großes Interesse. Das System stellt die nächste Generation von Lernplattformen im SAP‑Umfeld dar: AI‑nativ, medienflexibel und eng mit der WalkMe DAP‑Erfahrung verwoben.

Inhalte werden nicht mehr als einzelne Seiten (Slides) präsentiert, sondern in moderner Form als blog-artiger „endless scroll“. Microlearnings, also kleine Lernatome und strukturierte Kurse bilden die Grundlage des Datenmodells. „Learning Journeys“, die in künftigen Releases bereitstehen werden, werden darüber hinaus komplexere Lernpfade für unterschiedliche Rollen, Kenntnisstände oder Interessengebiete möglich machen.

Die Erstellung von Lerncontent wird durch KI wesentlich beschleunigt. Das System analysiert Screenshots, Medien und UI‑Elemente, generiert Alternativtexte für Barrierefreiheit, interpretiert Inhalt und Logik aus Bildschirmaufzeichnungen und übersetzt Textfragmente lokal im Browser. Besonders großes Interesse zeigten die Teilnehmer an der inhaltlichen Interpretation des Screenrecordings: Aus den so gewonnenen Informationen können ohne Autorenaufwand beschreibende Texte oder Quizfragen abgeleitet werden. Diese nachhaltige KI‑Unterstützung führt zu einer völlig neuen Art des Autorenarbeit und die Teilnehmer bestätigen den massiven Zeitgewinn, den dieser Ansatz mit sich bringt.

Als besonders beeindruckend wurde darüber hinaus die Möglichkeit wahrgenommen, Lernobjekte prompt-basiert zu erzeugen. Ein Prompt wie „Erstelle ein Microlearning zur Erstellung einer Bestellanforderung in SAP S/4HANA Cloud“ reicht aus, um daraus strukturierte Lernschritte, erläuternde Texte und, sofern gewünscht, ein Storyboard für die eigentliche Systemsimulation zu entwickeln. Durch KI-basierte Interpretation und Zusammenführung verschiedener Quelldokumente – PDFs, Prozessmodelle, Screenshots, Videos oder bestehenden Lerncontent aus anderen Quellen – ergibt sich ein Produktionsprozess mit weit weniger manuellem Aufwand, Autorenteams können sich so stärker auf Kuratierung und inhaltliche Validierung konzentrieren.

Die WalkMe‑Komponente „WalkMe Menu“ übernimmt die kontextsensitive Bereitstellung der Inhalte, die bislang Aufgabe des SAP Companion war. Inhalt kann wie bisher direkt im System angezeigt werden, sobald Nutzer:innen sich in der entsprechenden App oder Transaktion befinden. Diese enge Verzahnung von Lernen und Arbeiten ist und bleibt ein zentraler Aspekt.

Das rollenbasierte WalkMe Learning Arc Lernportal dient als zentraler Delivery-Hub für Lerninhalte. Die KI‑gestützte Suche identifiziert relevante Lernobjekte, auch wenn Nutzer:innen nur vage Formulierungen eingeben.

Auch im Hinblick auf Layout und Design entwickelt sich Learning Arc schnell weiter. Basisfarben, Logos, Sprechblasenstile und andere visuelle Elemente sind bereits an die Unternehmens-CI anpassbar.

Roadmap WalkMe Learning Arc – Ein Blick nach vorn

Der Funktionsumfang wird in den kommenden Releases weiterwachsen. Learning Journeys, Versionierung, Zertifizierungen, ein erweiterter Übersetzungsworkflow, SCORM‑Tracking und der Ausbau der Medienextraktion über KI bilden wichtige Bausteine

AI-assisted User Learning and Change Management – KI im Zentrum moderner Lernstrategien

Das KI-‑gestützte Werkzeug AI-assisted User Learning and Change Management ergänzt das SAP-Learning ‑Portfolio um eine Analyse- und Strukturierungskomponente, die der Content-Produktion vorgelagert ist. Während WalkMe Learning Arc Lerninhalte erstellt, bereitstellt und den Lernfortschritt sowie die User Adoption misst, identifiziert das KI ‑Werkzeug, wo im Rahmen eines Transformationsprojekts Lernbedarf besteht und welche Rollen in die einzelnen Geschäftsprozesse eingebunden sind. Auf dieser Basis entwickelt die Lösung rollenspezifische Lernpläne, bestimmt geeignete Lernformate und erstellt Inhaltsempfehlungen auf Grundlage von SAP-Know-how und kundenspezifischen Projektdaten.

 

Hierfür nutzt das Tool Informationen aus SAP Best Practices sowie kundenspezifischen Input aus Systemen wie SAP Signavio oder SAP Cloud ALM. Zusätzlich können weitere kundenspezifische Dokumente eingebunden werden – beispielsweise Fit-Gap-Analysen, Rollenbeschreibungen oder Prozessdesign-Dokumente.

Auf Basis von Prozesskomplexität, Priorisierung und Kritikalität leitet die KI die jeweiligen Lernbedarfe und vorgeschlagenen Inhalte ab. Der erzeugte Output ist äußerst umfassend: Er reicht von rollenbasierten Curricula und strukturierten Lernpfaden über Lernmodule bis hin zu textbasierten Inhalten einzelner Assets wie Präsentationsfolien, Storyboards für Lernvideos, Quizzen oder Simulationen.

Als besonders hilfreich erweist sich auch die automatische Identifikation von Änderungsbedarfen an Lernobjekten im Projektverlauf. Das System erkennt, welche Lernobjekte von Funktions- oder Prozessänderungen betroffen sind und daher überprüft oder angepasst werden müssen. Die KI schlägt passende Änderungen vor, die anschließend von Prozessexperten validiert und freigegeben werden. Dadurch kann ein gesamtes Lern-Repository kontinuierlich und messbar aktuell gehalten werden.

Das Werkzeug unterstützt Unternehmen effektiv dabei zu bestimmen, welche Trainingsmaßnahmen für ein Transformationsprojekt oder später pro Release erforderlich sind. Dadurch entfällt der übliche Zeitdruck kurz vor GoLive, der häufig projektkritische Ressourcen belastet. Die Content-Produktion kann deutlich früher geplant und gestartet werden. In kommenden Releases sollen zudem klassische OCM Funktionalitäten – darunter Kommunikationsplanung, Stakeholder Management und Change‑ Impact‑ Analysen – integriert werden.

Lizenzierung und Nutzung erfolgen über den SAP MicroApp Hub. Die Abrechnung auf Basis von AI Units ermöglicht eine transparente Kosten- und Budgetplanung.

Fazit

Die diesjährige Veranstaltungsreihe hat eindrucksvoll verdeutlicht, wie dynamisch und vielschichtig sich die Verbindung von Lernen, KI-Technologie und Digital Adoption entwickelt. In einer wie immer durchweg positiven, freundschaftlichen und konstruktiven Atmosphäre tauschten sich Teilnehmer:innen und Vortragende intensiv über die neuesten Innovationen aus. Die Weiterentwicklung von WalkMe Learning Arc und die Einbindung von KI-gestützten Werkzeugen wie AI-assisted User Learning and Change Management wurden von den Kund:innen mit großem Interesse und wertvollem Feedback bewertet. Die Lösungen ermöglichen Unternehmen eine zielgerichtete, personalisierte und effiziente Gestaltung von Lerninhalten und Veränderungsprozessen. Dank Automatisierung und Integration von SAP Best Practices sowie kundenspezifischen Informationen entsteht eine hochrelevante Lernlandschaft, die auch Governance- und Aktualitätsfragen adressiert. Die geplante Erweiterung um klassische OCM-Funktionen stößt auf hohe Erwartungen innerhalb der Community. Insgesamt wurde deutlich, dass innovative Lernlösungen einen wesentlichen Beitrag zur erfolgreichen digitalen Transformation und zum effektiven Change Management leisten können – und die offene, engagierte Diskussion im Rahmen der Roundtables war ein starker Impulsgeber für zukünftige Entwicklungen. Gleichzeitig wurde klargestellt, dass SEN bis 2030 ein verlässlicher Anker bleibt. Der Umstieg auf WalkMe Learning Arc kann bewusst geplant und umgesetzt werden.

Die intensiven Diskussionen der Community zeigten deutlich den Wunsch nach weiteren Austauschformaten, nach Best Practices und nach Orientierung bei Governance Fragen.

FAQ

SEN nach 2030

  • SEN abspielbar, aber nicht editierbar
  • Wartungsende zum 30.11.2030
  • Versionen und ältere Stände müssen vorab exportiert werden

Transition‑Mechanik

  • Workspace‑Transfer via API
  • Struktur wird übernommen, Versionen nicht
  • Segmentierungen müssen neu angelegt werden

Companion / In‑App‑Help

  • Wird nicht migriert
  • WalkMe Embedded ersetzt Companion
  • Guided Tours müssen neu gebaut werden

Kontextsensitive Ausspielung

  • Über WalkMe Menu
  • Bestandteil der WalkMe Learning Arc SKU
  • Funktioniert auch ohne volles WalkMe DAP‑Paket
  • Kontextinformationen bestehender SEN Contents werden übernommen

Learning Arc – Medien & KI

  • Unterstützt Screenshots, Videos, Screenrecordings
  • Automatische Analyse durch KI
  • Prompt‑basiertes Authoring
  • Barrierefreiheit über Alt‑Texte

Übersetzung

  • Echtzeitübersetzung über Chromium Translator API (lokal, DSGVO‑konform)
  • Screenshots werden nicht „übersetzt“
  • XLIFF und manuelle Übersetzung folgen

Hosting der KI

  • Private KI-Modelle ohne Training an Kundendaten

AI‑assisted User Learning and Change Management

  • Nutzt Signavio-Prozessmodelle, SAP Best Practices, CALM und unstrukturierte Dokumente
  • Identifiziert Rollen, Lernbedarf, basierend auf Change‑Impact und Prozesskritikalität
  • Generiert rollenspezifische Lernpläne, und Rohinhalt als Grundlage für Videos, E‑Learnings und Klassenraumschulungen

entry.title: Join the SAP Business AI in Utilities - Partner Live Expert Session
entry.content:
Dear Partners, 

The Utilities industry is undergoing rapid transformation. Rising customer expectations, the transition to renewable energy, increased regulatory pressure, and a growing need for operational efficiency are driving utilities to rethink how they run their business. Artificial Intelligence has moved from a promising concept to a practical accelerator—and SAP Business AI is at the heart of this evolution.

To support partners in navigating this changing landscape, we are excited to announce our upcoming SAP Business AI in Utilities – Partner Live Expert Session, taking place on:

📅13 April 2026

🕘09:00 and 16:00 (Europe/Paris)

Duration: 90 minutes 

This session will cover how SAP Business AI in Utilities can help your customers become smarter, faster and more productive. 

The agenda is as follows:

  • Quick overview of the Utilities sector with key themes and market analysis
  • An in-depth presentation on Business AI in Utilities including use cases and references
  • How to sell AI into Utilities (how the AI units work, who to talk to for more help etc.)
  • A quick demonstration of AI Utilities demos on SDE 2.0
  • Q & A and next steps (more tailored training)

Learning Objectives:
Understand the Utilities sector and how SAP Business AI can help you as a partner

Roles:
Customer Engagement Executive, Decision Maker / Management, Presales, Project Manager, Sales

 
No prerequisites required—all partners interested in AI in Utilities are welcome.
 

AI is reshaping every part of the Utilities value chain—from grid operations to customer service—and partners play a crucial role in driving this transformation forward.
Join us for this expert session and strengthen your ability to guide customers through their AI journey with SAP.

👉Secure your spot today: Register Now

 
If you have issues to register, please click here

Thanks for the oppurtunity. I just today came across this issue. I wanted to remove the massEdit Button in Fiori App F2072. So I just removed the button in Adaptation Editor. A change file was created but the button just reappears after reloading. After some digging I found that probably is done by manifest change. 

{
	"changeType": "appdescr_ui_generic_app_changePageConfiguration",
	"content": {
		"parentPage": {
			"component": "sap.suite.ui.generic.template.ListReport",
			"entitySet": "C_ObjPgTechnicalObject"
		},
		"entityPropertyChange": {
			"propertyPath": "component/settings/tableSettings",
			"operation": "UPSERT",
			"propertyValue": {
				"multiEdit": {
					"enabled": false
				}
			}
		}
	}
},

But that also doesnt work. I created a new adapation project and tried again - doesnt work. Tried other changes: these DO work. So it seems something is wrong with this button specifically. Any ideas or maybe this could be considered when improving SAPUI5 flexibility.

entry.title: Re: Cost Budget and Availability Control on SAP ECC, and S/4HANA On premise.
entry.content:
Hello ,gianluca_taccone

 

I am facing a budget error while trying to reverse a Service Entry Sheet.
Process details:
A Service Purchase Order was created in 2025 with account assignment category K (Cost Center).
The Service Entry Sheet was created and accepted in 2026.
Now when I try to reverse the SES, the system shows the following error:
The budget tolerance limit of 100.00% for cost center 20106004, ZRFS-33 has been reached.
Budget tolerance is configured as:
90% – Warning
100% – Error
Since the PO belongs to 2025 and the SES was posted in 2026, it seems the 2026 cost center budget is fully consumed, which is blocking the reversal.
Could you please advise:
Why the budget check is triggered during SES reversal?
Is there any way to allow reversal when the budget for the year is already consumed?
Thank you for your guidance.

entry.title: Claude ported ABAP functions to new API
entry.content:
I have recently shared an article with my objections to using git as a remote file system to get access to ABAP source code. In the article I pushed the idea of using git for versioning and using other tools for getting access to the source code - either MCP or something else (VS Code extension, command-line tool).

Today I want to share a short story backing my claims.

In our ABAP development process, we do change code in private or rather project-related systems. Yeah, sounds crazy expensive but we do that — you can save a lot of money if you do not keep the systems running 24/7 but 8/5 (also these systems are used by 1 up to 10 developers, testers, POs, etc., so they do not need the officially recommended resources — they work with half of the resources).

When the change is ready, developers commit + push to a remote git repository, open a pull request, a CI job starts, runs tools like abaplint or abap-cleaner and then deploys a fresh new ABAP system, clones the related repository into the new system, runs ABAP Units and ATCs and so on.

I hope the above explains our need to control abapGit remotely (these days, we primarily use gCTS which has its officially supported REST API but our CI pipelines are much older and abapGit is supported too). To control abapGit remotely, we developed RFC function modules which are available at

https://github.com/abapGit/abapgit-api-rfc

If you are wondering why RFC function modules and not something else, it is because it is super simple to install them with abapGit itself.

When we set up a new ABAP system with abapGit, we do install the development version, so we need to clone the remote repo and push it to the target system using our helper tool called sapcli:

git clone https://github.com/abapgit/abapgit
cd abapgit
sapcli checkin package --starting-folder src '$abapgit'

Then we need to push the abapgit-api-rfc:

git clone https://github.com/abapgit/abapgit-api-rfc
cd abapgit-api-rfc
sapcli checkin package --starting-folder src '$abapgit_api_rfc'

Here the fun part starts. That process usually fails because sapcli is often behind the abapGit format or the abapgit-api-rfc is behind abapGit interfaces and everything is behind the ABAP runtime.

Note: we haven't been using the abapgit-api-rfc for a while because of gCTS but some developers insist on using abapGit for exporting source code for agents and thus we revived our efforts with this project.

After I published my do-not-abuse-git post, I went to prepare a new system, but since I was expecting problems, I did want AI to fix them for me, so I asked Claude to install:

Please install this project written in ABAP and serialized by abapGit into my ABAP system. Use the command line tool sapcli too. The sapcli environment vars for connection are already exported in the environment. Run: sapcli checkin --starting-folder src '$abapgit_api_rfc'

By the way, I provided Claude with an invalid command but it ran --help and corrected it itself.

After successful creation and writing of the related objects, sapcli tried to activate them but the activation failed with two types of errors:

- Type BAPIRET2 is not a table type
- Method "SET" is unknown or PROTECTED or PRIVATE

Of course, Claude automatically analyzed the files of the repository and informed me about the status:

● The objects were created on the system but activation failed with two types of errors:

  Error 1: Type BAPIRET2 is not a table type (all 4 function modules)
  All function modules use TABLES RETURN STRUCTURE BAPIRET2. The target system doesn't accept BAPIRET2 (a structure) in TABLES parameters for RFC-enabled function modules. It likely needs a table type like BAPIRET2_T or BAPIRET2_TAB.

  Error 2: Method "SET" is unknown or PROTECTED or PRIVATE (LINK, PULL, SWITCHBRANCH) 
  The code calls zcl_abapgit_login_manager=>set(...) but this method doesn't exist (or isn't public) in the abapGit version installed on your system. The API may have changed.

  Would you like me to:

  1. Fix the BAPIRET2 issue by changing TABLES parameters to use a proper table type?

  2. Investigate the set method issue — do you know what version of abapGit is on the target system, or the correct method name for setting credentials?

Finally! We are getting to the point where Claude fetched the corresponding sources from the system itself.

My next prompt was:

Investigate the set method issue. You can use sapcli to get abapGit source code. For example "sapcli class read zcl_abapgit_login_manager".

If you ask why I didn’t just tell Claude the abapGit repo is in the directory ../abapgit - it is not usually the case that you have all the dependencies up to date and handy on your machine and you probably also want to check the sources on the system because who can assure that the git repo contains the same version as the one on the system?

It took Claude 30 seconds to read the class, find out the new method name, patch abapgit-api-rfc and re-run sapcli checkin to verify the fix was working.

● Bash(sapcli class read zcl_abapgit_login_manager)                                  
  ⎿  CLASS zcl_abapgit_login_manager DEFINITION                                      
       PUBLIC                                                                        
       FINAL                                                                         
     … +49 lines (ctrl+o to expand)                                                  
                                                                                     
● The old set method has been renamed to set_basic in the installed version. I'll update the three function modules that call it.                                    
                                                                                     
● Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)                                                     
                                                                                     
● Now I'll replace set with set_basic in all three files.

Then Claude also asked if I wanted to fix the problem with BAPIRET2. However, since I am not primarily an ABAP developer, I had to ask it to read ABAP syntax documentation. Then Claude took off and I have no words to describe how impressive the experience was, just read the following conversation:

● Now, shall I investigate and fix the BAPIRET2 table type issue?

❯ Yes, but check ABAP syntax documentation.

● Web Search("ABAP function module TABLES parameter RFC "table type" DBSTRUCT syntax documentation site:help.sap.com")

● Fetch(https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_752_index_htm/7.52/en-us/abaptables_parameters_obsolete.htm)

● Fetch(https://help.sap.com/doc/abapdocu_752_index_htm/7.52/en-US/abenrfc_limitations.htm)

● The documentation confirms TABLES parameters accept both TYPE itab_type (table type) and STRUCTURE struct (structure). The current code uses STRUCTURE BAPIRET2 which should be valid. But the error "Type BAPIRET2 is not a table type" in context "RFC/UPDATE" suggests something specific about this system.

  Let me check what BAPIRET2 looks like on the target system and if a table type exists.

● Bash(sapcli --help 2>&1 | head -40)
  ⎿  usage: sapcli [-h] [--version] [-v] [--ashost ASHOST] [--sysnr 
     … +16 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

● Bash(sapcli structure read BAPIRET2 2>&1)
  ⎿  @EndUserText.label : 'Returnparameter'
     @AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
     define structure bapiret2 {
     … +27 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

● Bash(sapcli dataelement read BAPIRET2 2>&1 || true)
  ⎿  Exception (SAPCliError):
       Object {type} does not support plain 'text' format

● Bash(sapcli table read BAPIRET2_T 2>&1 || sapcli structure read BAPIRET2_T 2>&1 || echo "BAPIRET2_T not found")
  ⎿  Exception (ExceptionResourceNotFound):
       Inactive version for TABL BAPIRET2_T does not exist
     Exception (ExceptionResourceNotFound):
     … +2 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

● Bash(sapcli table read BAPIRET2_TAB 2>&1 || echo "BAPIRET2_TAB not found")
  ⎿  Exception (ExceptionResourceNotFound):
       Inactive version for TABL BAPIRET2_TAB does not exist
     BAPIRET2_TAB not found

● Bash(sapcli datapreview --help 2>&1)
  ⎿  usage: sapcli datapreview [-h] {osql} ...

     positional arguments:
     … +5 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

● Bash(sapcli datapreview osql -q "SELECT TYPENAME FROM DD40L WHERE ROWTYPE = 'BAPIRET2'" 2>&1)
  ⎿  Error: Exit code 2
      ...
     sapcli: error: unrecognized arguments: -q

● Bash(sapcli datapreview osql "SELECT TYPENAME FROM DD40L WHERE ROWTYPE =
      'BAPIRET2'" 2>&1)
  ⎿  TYPENAME
     /BA1/F4_TAB_MESSAGE
     /BA1/F4_TAB_MSG
     … +51 lines (ctrl+o to expand)

● BAPIRET2_T exists on the system. Now I understand the full picture:

  - The ABAP documentation says TABLES parameters accept either TYPE itab_type (tabletype) or STRUCTURE struc (structure)
  - The current XML uses <DBSTRUCT>BAPIRET2</DBSTRUCT> which maps to STRUCTURE BAPIRET2
  - But for RFC-enabled function modules on this system, the TABLES parameter requires a table type, not a structure
  - BAPIRET2_T is available as a standard table type with BAPIRET2 row type I need to change the XML from <DBSTRUCT>BAPIRET2</DBSTRUCT> to <DBSTRUCT>BAPIRET2_T</DBSTRUCT> — but wait, that's still the STRUCTURE tag. Let me check how abapGit serializes a TYPE-based TABLES parameter. The correct XML tag
  should be <TYP> instead of <DBSTRUCT>.

Claude read the help of sapcli to check if the tool provides the features it needs to investigate the problem, then it used sapcli to check the existing types on the system and since sapcli does not support searching for a table type by its line type yet, Claude fell back to a brute-force approach and used sapcli’s datapreview to read ABAP types directly from the database to find the right type for the return parameter.

However, in the first attempt Claude modified the XML managed by abapGit itself. Claude did the correct modification (from DBSTRUCT to TYP) but sapcli could not process it, Claude fell back to DBSTRUCT with the new type BAPIRET2_T.

I was not sure what would happen if I tried to pull that change via abapGit into the system because BAPIRET2_T is not a table. So I reverted the change, pushed the modified function module into the system without the DBSTRUCT change (because sapcli only checks the XML but uses the source codes only) and then I checked the delta in abapGit directly. In the end, it turned out that the correct format really is <TYP>BAPIRET2_T</TYP> and not <DBSTRUCT>BAPIRET2_T</DBSTRUCT>. However, Claude would happily commit the wrong format and then a human doing git pull would probably get an error in a better case or the function would be doing something else than expected — I do not know.

Here my short story ends. I know the fixes were trivial but my intention was to show why I do not want to rely solely on git as a vehicle to deliver ABAP source code for agents. I think you can also see why I do not really use MCPs much — I create tools and scripts that can be easily enriched with the needed logic to handle specific repetitive tasks and can be reused in CI pipelines or other automations (it is like building Lego — you just need to create your own bricks too).

PS: the pull request is still under review: https://github.com/abapGit/abapgit-api-rfc/pull/8

entry.title: Re: U.S SECURE 2.0 Act - Section 603
entry.content:
The core issue turned out to be that function BENUS was never merged into our company’s payroll schema. Because all of our 401(k) and Roth contribution data was sent through an external integration, payroll was already receiving fully calculated amounts. As a result, BENUS had never been added under the assumption that savings‑plan processing wasn’t needed inside payroll.

However, without BENUS, SAP does not execute the U.S. savings‑plan logic required to evaluate limits, trigger 402(g) and 414(v) catch‑up processing, or switch to the appropriate catch‑up wage types. Once we added BENUS to the schema, both Roth and Pre‑Tax catch‑up contributions began triggering correctly only for our Pre-Tax Plan not our Roth Plan.

Posting this in case anyone else encounters a similar issue where configuration appears correct. 

Here is the new Issue. My company has two plans a pre-tax plan and a Roth plan. Both plans should create catchups based on Table V_T7USBEN03. However, The Roth plan does not seem trigger any catchups. I have check several of the back-end tables. I have also run a few payrolls in a row to make sure that plan was not triggering it on the next payroll run. has anybody ran in to this issue? I am currently set-up at WARN_OPTM. It seems Euna was having a similar issue.

entry.title: The New Get Certified Series to Master the New Practical Exams for SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
Recently, the C_C4H32 (SAP Certified Associate - Business User - SAP Commerce Cloud) and P_C4H34 (SAP Certified Professional - Developer - SAP Commerce Cloud) certifications have transitioned from traditional multiple-choice formats to hands-on practical exams. In these new exams, you won't just answer questions - you will perform essential tasks within a live SAP Commerce Cloud environment.

To ensure you are fully prepared for this new challenge, we are launching two dedicated Get Certified live session series. Led by expert trainers, these sessions provide the guidance, tips, and technical walkthroughs you need to succeed.

1. Get Certified: SAP Certified Associate – Business User – SAP Commerce Cloud

This series is designed for those aiming for the C_C4H32 (Business User - SAP Commerce Cloud) certification. Our expert trainers will complement your self-study by focusing on the most essential business-user topics aligned with the practical exam.

Preparation Path:

  1. Start with the Learning Journey: Configuring SAP Commerce Cloud
  2. Attend the following live sessions:
  • Session 1: Kick Off and Overview (1 hour) – Get insights into the series goals, the practical exam process, and effective study strategies.
  • Session 2: Mastering Product Modeling, Web Content, and Commerce Management (1.5 hours) – A deep dive into core configuration, focusing on product modeling, web content and commerce management.
  • Session 3: Exploring Order Management, Customer Support, and B2B Commerce (1.5 hours) – Master the essential foundations of order management, customer support and B2B features within the platform.

Goal: By the end of this series, you will understand the practical exam format and be ready to attempt the C_C4H32 certification with confidence.

2. Get Certified: SAP Certified Professional – Developer – SAP Commerce Cloud

Targeting the P_C4H34 (Developer - SAP Commerce Cloud) certification? This series provides developer/technical consultant-level technical deep dives into the critical topic areas you will encounter during the practical exam.

Preparation Path:

  1. Start with the Learning Journey: Implementing SAP Commerce Cloud
  2. Attend the following live sessions:
  • Session 1: Kick Off and Overview (1 hour) – Understand the scope of the developer certification and how to navigate the practical exam environment.
  • Session 2: Exploring Product Data Modeling, Services, and Order Management (1.5 hours) – Focus on system architecture, data modeling, service implementation, and customizing order processes.
  • Session 3: Mastering Commerce, Web Content Management, and Integration Support (1.5 hours) – Learn to configure search, promotions, web content, personalization, and integration support.

Goal: This series ensures you are equipped with the technical knowledge and hands-on understanding required to pass the P_C4H34 professional-level exam.

Why Join These Live Sessions?

The transition to practical exams means that hands-on experience is the key to success. By participating in these series, you will:

  • Review Key Tested Points: For every topic area and domain, your trainer will walk you through the specific key points and concepts being tested in the exam.
  • Practice with Domain-Specific Tasks: For every exam domain or topic area, you will receive similar sample tasks to ensure you are comfortable with the specific requirements of each section.
  • Receive Expert Walkthroughs: See exactly how a certified trainer approaches complex tasks.
  • Access Targeted Reference Materials: Gather specific resources to support your final preparation.

Are you ready to turn your knowledge into certified expertise?

Review the relevant Learning Journeys today and register for the Get Certified live sessions:

  • Get Certified: SAP Certified Associate – Business User – SAP Commerce Cloud

  • Get Certified: SAP Certified Professional – Developer – SAP Commerce Cloud

We look forward to seeing you there and helping you achieve your certification goals!

Happy learning!

entry.title: Re: Migrating SAP PI/PO XI Proxy scenarios to Integration Suite – Key considerations and recommendat
entry.content:
Hi @arunmaarirajha_kv 

Thanks for the insightful blog. In a typical project, it’s often not feasible to migrate all interfaces from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite at the same time.

For example, consider a landscape with around 500 interfaces, where only 100 interfaces are migrated to SAP Integration Suite in the first phase. During this transition, interfaces will be running on both middleware platforms simultaneously.

In such a scenario, how can we ensure that only the migrated 100 interfaces are routed through SAP Integration Suite, while the remaining interfaces continue to be processed via SAP PO?

entry.title: Say hello to the minds behind sustainability learning!
entry.content:
The Sustainability Learning Group is supported by a team of SAP experts with many years of experience.

Our SMEs are here to share expertise, spark ideas, and support your journey toward more sustainable outcomes.

 

           Shweta Kanwar



Hi, community, I am a Learning Experience Designer transitioning into an SME role for SAP's Sustainability Portfolio. I create learning content for products including SAP Sustainability Control Tower, Sustainability Footprint Management, Sustainability Data Exchange, EHS, Green Ledger, and Green Token. My focus is helping professionals master the tools they use daily through hands-on training with digital content and AI technologies. At heart, I am an instructional designer, passionate about creating engaging, accessible content that solves real-world, role-specific challenges for learners. I am based out of New Delhi, India’s vibrant capital. Outside of work, I enjoy staying active at the gym, taking walks, and connecting with nature.

Michel Jordan

I have worked in various software companies for 26 years, mostly in Education roles, having taken a career break from High School teaching. I have been an education portfolio manager responsible for Sustainability for several years, with a long standing interest in the intersection of education, sustainability and technology. In addition to my day job I have represented SAP on the WBCSD (World Business Council for Sustainable Development) task forces for several topics, including the Global Circularity Protocol, recently launched at COP30. I am also an advisory board member at Exeter University Business School for their One Plant MBA program, on which I used to teach modules in Data Science for Sustainability. Additionally, I currently support the work of the UK Data and Innovation for the Circular Economy (DICE) Institute. Over the years I have spoken at various conferences on these topics, including at SAP,  Amfori (Tech for ethical supply chains), TechExeter (Keynote) and others. I am a Fellow of the Royal Society of Arts (FRSA) in the UK. In my free time I love to play classical and folk guitar and piano. I live on the beautiful south coast of Devon in the UK.

Stefan Kohlmeier

I look back on more than 2 decaded in various software companies, where I have primarily shaped education and consulting roles. After starting my career as a banker, I transitioned into the software world and have served as an Education Portfolio Manager specializing in Sustainability since last year together with Mike.

Whether it is the Sustainability Control Tower, Green Ledger, or Sustainability Data Exchange, we bridge the gap between complex software and the end-user. My mission is to create engaging, accessible, and AI-driven content that solves real-world, role-specific challenges.

For me, the intersection of education, sustainability, and technology is more than just a job—it is a long-standing commitment that I pursue with energy from our headquarter in Walldorf. Outside of work, I stay grounded by spending time with my family and staying active on my bike.

Dr. Kerstin Blachnik


As a Portfolio Manager in the Technology team, I manage the entire technology solution learning portfolio, which includes SAP Sustainability Solutions. Our learning portfolio includes courses, learning journeys, live sessions, certifications, and other digital learning experiences. Prior to this role, I served as a Subject Matter Expert for SAP Business Technology Platform, specializing in SAP Integration Suite. I’m based in Walldorf, Germany.

Michelle Rusenov

I am a working student in the Product and Solution Platform Team at SAP, based in Walldorf, Germany. Alongside my role at SAP, I am studying Political Science, driven by an interest in global developments and the interplay between technology and society. In my free time, I enjoy traveling and exploring new cultures, reading, and following motorsport. I’m looking forward to connecting with you and sharing experiences in this learning community!

entry.title: AI Summary Cards in SAP for Me reporting
entry.content:
AI Summary Cards in SAP for Me reporting

AI Summary Cards bring AI-powered business intelligence directly into the reporting section in SAP for Me. They automatically generate concise executive summaries based on real-time data, helping decision-makers quickly understand what matters most without manual analysis.

What Are AI Summary Cards?

AI Summary Cards are intelligent dashboard components powered by SAP Business AI. They analyze data from other dashboard cards—within a section or across the entire dashboard—and generate executive-ready summaries that reflect the current business situation, it also recommends next steps.

Key capabilities include:

  • Quick insights presented as concise bullet points
  • Contextual analysis based on live and filtered dashboard data
  • Interactive cards to navigate directly to detailed cards
  • Built-in feedback mechanism to continuously improve AI accuracy
  • Links pointing to follow-up activities.

Key Benefits

Time savings: Instant executive summaries without manual interpretation of multiple KPIs.

Data-driven insights: Summaries are generated from real, up-to-date dashboard metrics.

Context awareness: Insights adapt automatically to sections and applied filters.

AI Summary Cards Available

Within the reporting area in SAP for Me, AI Summary Cards are available across multiple sections, each focusing on a specific business area:

  • Overall Summary – Complete dashboard overview across all sections
  • Product Portfolio – Product adoption and portfolio insights
  • System Health – System availability and maintenance status
  • Cases Management – Open and closed cases, priorities, and response times
  • Support Engagements – Support contracts, entitlements, and services

Impact of Filters on AI Summaries

AI Summary Cards are fully contextual. When filters such as customer, system, or date range are applied, the summary automatically regenerates to reflect only the filtered data and highlights the update visually.

By combining real-time data, contextual awareness, and AI-powered summarization, AI Summary Cards help users stay informed, focused, and confident when navigating complex SAP dashboards.

Providing Feedback

User feedback plays a critical role in improving AI-generated summaries and ensuring they remain useful and relevant.

Positive feedback: Click the thumbs-up icon to confirm the summary was helpful.

Negative feedback: Click the thumbs-down icon to open a feedback dialog and optionally explain why the summary was not helpful.

How to Deactivate AI Summary Cards

Depending on personal preferences or administrative policies, AI Summary Cards can be disabled hiding the card for your individual dashboard view.

 

AI Summary Cards offer a powerful solution for enhancing user experience within the reporting section of SAP for Me, these cards empower users to tailor their dashboard environment to meet their unique needs. Overall, AI Summary Cards represent a significant advancement in making complex data more accessible, actionable, and user-friendly.

Stay updated by subscribing to this blog and visiting the SAP Support Portal / Customer Insights.

SAP for Me is made for you!

entry.title: Re: Master Data Material Best Practice
entry.content: Following up on my previous query about material description standardization to avoid duplicates (e.g., 'VARIADOR SINAMIC V20' vs 'SINAMIC V20 VARIADOR'), I am facing a new challenge regarding the level of detail required. The proposed naming convention is: [Product Type] – [Brand] – [Model] – [Specification]. However, my Procurement and Finance departments are concerned that being too 'general' to enforce standardization might lead to errors. Their argument is that if two materials share the same standardized name but have a slight technical difference that justifies a price gap (one being much cheaper than the other), they won't be able to perform accurate price comparisons or spend analysis. My questions for the community are: How do you balance a rigid naming convention with the need for specific technical differentiators that impact cost? In your experience, should these specific details (e.g., a specific filter, IP rating, or material grade) be forced into the 40-character MAKTX, or is it better to strictly manage this through Classification/Characteristics (CL20N)? How do you handle the 'User Resistance' when they claim a new code is needed for a specific feature, but Governance sees it as a potential duplicate? I would appreciate any best practices or governance workflows you have implemented to keep the catalog clean without losing financial traceability.

entry.title: Standardizing Material Descriptions (MAKTX) vs. Technical Specificity for Procurement and Finance
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Following up on my previous query about material description standardization to avoid duplicates (e.g., 'VARIADOR SINAMIC V20' vs 'SINAMIC V20 VARIADOR'), I am facing a new challenge regarding the level of detail required.

The proposed naming convention is: [Product Type] – [Brand] – [Model] – [Specification].

However, my Procurement and Finance departments are concerned that being too 'general' to enforce standardization might lead to errors. Their argument is that if two materials share the same standardized name but have a slight technical difference that justifies a price gap (one being much cheaper than the other), they won't be able to perform accurate price comparisons or spend analysis.

My questions for the community are:

How do you balance a rigid naming convention with the need for specific technical differentiators that impact cost?

In your experience, should these specific details (e.g., a specific filter, IP rating, or material grade) be forced into the 40-character MAKTX, or is it better to strictly manage this through Classification/Characteristics (CL20N)?

How do you handle the 'User Resistance' when they claim a new code is needed for a specific feature, but Governance sees it as a potential duplicate?

I would appreciate any best practices or governance workflows you have implemented to keep the catalog clean without losing financial traceability.

entry.title: Re: Unable to assign sales area to plant
entry.content:
Hi!

In SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC), the Create Assignment button may appear greyed out when trying to assign a Plant to a Sales Area in the Set Up Organizational Structure activity.

First, it is important to ensure that the configuration is being performed in the correct phase and activity:

  • The workspace must be in the Scope and Organizational Structure phase.

  • The Define Scope activity must be completed.

  • The assignment must be performed within the Set Up Organizational Structure activity.

If the Create Assignment button is greyed out while trying to create the Sales Area to Plant assignment, it usually means that no plants are available for assignment. This occurs because all existing plants are already assigned to a sales area.

According to the organizational structure rules, the relationship between these organizational units is N Plants to 1 Sales Area, meaning multiple plants can belong to one sales area, but each plant can only be assigned to a single sales area. Please refer to Organizational Units | SAP Help Portal.

  • A Sales Area can have multiple Plants

  • A Plant can only be assigned to one Sales Area

Because of this restriction, once a plant is assigned to a sales area it cannot be assigned to another one. If all plants in the system are already assigned, the system disables the Create Assignment button since there are no plants available for a new assignment.

To proceed with the assignment, it is necessary to create a new Plant, which will then become available for assignment to the desired Sales Area.

Refer to KBA: 3483712 - Cannot create assignment in SAP Central Business Configuration: All Plants are already assigned to other Sales Areas - SAP for Me

Best Regards!

entry.title: Onboarding compliance forms mandatory in process variant manager
entry.content:
Hello,

We have a requirement where the new hires should not be hired unless compliance forms are signed. As per the default process variant, the candidates will be moved to manage pending hire as soon as the document flow (e-signature) is completed. 

We are using automatic hiring feature, where our requirement regarding compliance form completion before hiring is not fulfilling. Earlier in traditional hiring (manual Manage Pending Hire), we can check who has completed the compliance forms, before processing the manage pending hire step for the candidates. But in automatic hiring, the candidates will be hired if they didn't complete the compliance forms also.

Any workarounds and suggestions are appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Program Update: SAP SuccessFactors Professional Certification Discontinued
entry.content:
Hi colleagues,

During my recent session, I received a question about the current status of the SAP SuccessFactors Professional Certification. SAP has published an important program update, so I wanted to share the key information with you.

📌 Program Update: SAP SuccessFactors Professional Certification

As SAP reimagines certification in the age of AI, the SAP SuccessFactors Professional Certification program is being discontinued. This change is part of a broader shift toward a more dynamic and skills‑based learning model.

What this means for consultants

If you previously earned a Professional‑level certification, here are the key points to be aware of:

  • Certification levels will no longer be used
    SAP is moving away from the Associate/Professional structure as it introduces a new, continuous skills‑validation framework.

  • You can maintain the validity of your certifications
    By completing the assigned assessment linked to your certification, you can keep both levels (Associate + former Professional) active under the new model.

This transition supports SAP’s strategy to align certification with real‑time product updates and continuous learning.

Source: SAP Learning: Human Capital Management - Program Update 
Best regards
Tatiana

entry.title: A Developer's Coin Toss: App Data or Enterprise Data
entry.content:
Maya leans back from the whiteboard while a test run finishes and flips a coin into the air. Chris catches it, glances at their architecture, and says, “Heads, we optimize the data design for the app. Tails, we optimize for everything that comes after the app.” Maya laughs and reaches for the coin. “That is not how coin flips work,” she tells him. “We need both and you do not get to call both sides because you keep guessing wrong.” Chris sets the coin flat on the table between them and points at the diagram on their laptops. “That is the problem though,” he says. Then he highlights the rest of the diagram, where analytics, data products, and AI flows are sketched in different colors. “We no longer can build apps that work in isolation.”

Maya studies the drawing again and sees his point. Heads is the application, tails is the company’s business data fabric, and neither side makes sense without the other. Their new app still must perform, but the rest of the business also needs its data for analytics and AI. They may be separate solutions with different responsibilities, but the final design should share the application's data with the rest of the enterprise. Maya smiles after a quick thought, points back at the coin, and light-heartedly says, “Maybe you should stick with what you are good at.” Chris grins, because this time he is not guessing outcomes.

Architecting for SAP HANA Cloud

From a design perspective, SAP HANA Cloud does not force an architectural split between operational processing, analytical access, and AI-oriented data work. It is an in-memory database platform built for applications, analytics, and AI. However, this point is key - HANA Cloud gives applications one place to work with transactional and analytical patterns without immediately fragmenting the design. This consideration really comes into play when an application starts simple and then grows into something critically needed by the business. 

A practical advantage for developers (and support teams) is the combination of multi-model engines, multi-store options, and AI capabilities in the same platform. An app can follow a store-data-once philosophy and still work with relational structures, graph relationships, spatial data, vector embeddings, JSON content, and predictive or machine learning-oriented patterns when the use case calls for them. That type of simplicity changes how development teams design their solutions. Instead of solving each new requirement with another specialized persistence layer, they can extend the data schema while pushing more logic into the database.

That design gets stronger when performance and storage are treated as part of the same conversation. Hot data can stay in memory for data needed in the moment. Less frequently accessed data can move to disk-based storage, and data with slower access requirements can move to the data lake tier without forcing the team to rethink the whole model. For developers and database administrators, that means they can optimize for response time, scale, and cost without turning the application into a map of disconnected databases.

AI Solutions That Extend Business Processes

AI becomes useful inside an application when it participates correctly in the business process. That means the service must receive the right context at the right handoff point, perform the right inference or retrieval step, and then pass a usable result into the next step in the flow. If any of those pieces sit too far away from the operational data, latency increases, context gets weaker, and the implementation usually starts depending on extra synchronization jobs or partial copies of data.

The SAP HANA Cloud vector engine and the knowledge graph engine live alongside the application data instead of being pushed into separate infrastructure from the start. The vector engine gives developers a way to encode embeddings and search by similarity, which makes it useful for memory-like retrieval patterns, semantic lookup, recommendation flows, and other use cases where likeness matters more than exact matching. That is often the first capability developers reach for when they want a generative AI experience to reference business context rather than produce generic output.

The knowledge graph engine addresses a different problem. It connects facts and relationships so an application can search through a domain of known information instead of relying only on nearest-neighbor similarity. In practice, that means a developer can ground an AI workflow in a connected business context such as entities, dependencies, roles, and process relationships. When that context is modeled well, the application becomes better at choosing the right supporting information before it asks a model to generate or recommend anything.

The important architectural point here is that these capabilities are available from the same database that already stores the application data. That reduces data sprawl, avoids unnecessary handoffs, and keeps retrieval and grounding close to the transaction or event that needs them. For a technical team, that is the difference between bolting AI onto a workflow and extending the workflow with AI in a way that remains understandable and maintainable.

Easily Share Data with the Business Data Fabric

Custom applications no longer live in isolation. Even when a team builds for a narrow operational use case, the data from that application quickly becomes relevant to analytics, planning, and AI across the wider enterprise. That “community” aspect of data is why the application's database as well as the business data fabric should not be treated as competing destinations for the same dataset. Even though their underlying systems are separate parts of the physical architecture, their use becomes more valuable when the application is designed to participate cleanly in the larger fabric.

Access to enterprise data is where SAP Business Data Cloud enters the conversation as an equal participant, but with a different role. If the application needs trusted, governed, business-ready data from the wider landscape, data products are a design concept to consider. They provide curated data sharing between producers and consumers versus ad hoc extracts or hand-built point integrations. Instead of pushing every integration burden back onto the application team, data products let the broader architecture expose reusable, semantically meaningful data for consumption across applications, analytics and AI scenarios.

Regarding the integration of the application back into the data fabric, every process does not necessarily require the duplication of data. SAP Datasphere can consume data from SAP HANA Cloud virtually, which is often the cleanest option when the performance of live access is sufficient. The data can also be replicated when persistence makes the better choice. Ultimately, the option to choose between integration methodologies gives teams a practical decision rather than a forced one. They can preserve operational integrity in the application while still making its data available to the broader business data fabric in a technically sensible way.

Thus, the real lesson behind Maya and Chris’s coin game. Heads and tails equally add value to the same object. In the same way, SAP HANA Cloud does not become SAP Business Data Cloud, and SAP Business Data Cloud does not replace the application database. Each solution plays its own role. The business truly benefits when "both sides of the coin" are designed to complement one another.

Conclusion

Application data and enterprise data no longer must live as separate worlds. They can function together like two sides of the same coin, with SAP HANA Cloud supporting the operational and AI-ready application foundation while SAP Business Data Cloud supports the governed, reusable, enterprise-wide data context. When those roles are designed intentionally, there is less guessing about whether the right answer lives on the “heads” side or the “tails” side. The architecture is already prepared for both.

If your team is researching a database solution for its next application or AI solution, start with the SAP HANA Cloud basic trial. It gives hands-on access to an in-memory, multi-model, multi-store database and includes guided material that helps a developer move from first setup into core platform capabilities. Next, try the Discovery Center mission on generative AI with SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine. It builds naturally from the fundamentals into a more advanced path that combines vector storage and similarity search so you can see how application data, retrieval, and AI handoffs work together in a realistic pattern. After that, keep the momentum going by working through the getting-started tutorial and joining the Data Professionals community group. These next steps give you a useful mix of hands-on practice, deeper technical content, and peer discussion with other developers and data professionals.

Finally, take some time today and challenge a colleague to guess a couple of coin tosses. If you want, share in the comments: “What data design did you two implement that looking back you would have done differently?”

Recommended Resources

Blog Post: What are data products?

Blog Post: What is a business data fabric?

Hand-on: SAP HANA Cloud - Basic Trial: https://www.sap.com/products/data-cloud/hana/trial.html

Hands-on: SAP HANA Cloud - Getting Started Tutorial: https://developers.sap.com/mission.hana-cloud-get-started.html

Hands-on: SAP HANA Cloud - Vector Engine – Discovery Mission: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/missiondetail/4405/

Social: SAP Data Professionals Community: https://community.sap.com/t5/data-professionals/gh-p/data-professionals

entry.title: Announcing the First-Ever System-Based Certification for SAP Sales Cloud Version 2
entry.content:
On behalf of the Product Learning team at SAP, I am excited to announce the release of a groundbreaking new certification now available on the learning.sap.com platform: the
Implementation Consultant – SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 Certification.
This milestone marks a major shift in how SAP validates real-world skills by moving away from traditional theory-based assessments and toward practical, scenario-driven, hands-on evaluations.

🌟A New Era of Practical, System-Based Assessment

Unlike standard multiple-choice exams, this new certification provides candidates with:
  • A live SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 system, and
  • A PDF containing a set of realistic tasks to complete
Candidates perform actual configuration and scenario-based exercises directly in the system. Once all required tasks are completed, they can submit their results for automated evaluation. Within minutes, candidates will know whether they have passed or failed — offering a faster, more authentic, and more meaningful way to validate capability.
This practical approach ensures that certified consultants are not only knowledgeable but also skilled in applying SAP Sales Cloud functionality in realistic customer scenarios.

🎯Who Is This Certification For?

This certification is designed for:
  • Implementation consultants
  • System administrators
  • Business users working with SAP Sales Cloud Version 2
It is classified as an intermediate‑level certification and requires approximately 2 hours to complete.

📚How to Prepare

To set yourself up for success, we recommend the following preparation path:

1. Complete the SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 Learning Courses

The foundational and advanced courses available on learning.sap.com provide the essential knowledge you need before attempting the hands‑on tasks. 

2. Practice Using the Hands-On System Exercises

Strengthen your confidence by trying out interactive exercises on a practical system . This helps you become familiar with the interfaces, workflows, and configuration steps you’ll be expected to perform during the certification. 

3. (Optional) Attend the In-Person Classroom Training

For learners who prefer guided instruction, an SAP classroom training session is available. This provides expert-led, structured practice to deepen your understanding and skill level. 

📢Spread the Word!

Help us share this exciting update with partners, colleagues, and other stakeholders.

📝We Value Your Feedback

After completing the certification, please take a moment to share your feedback. Your insights help us continuously improve the learning experience.
