entry.title: Modernizing SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Modernizing SAP BW with SAP Business Data Cloud

 

Modernizing SAP BW is no longer a technical upgrade conversation—it has become a strategic inflection point for enterprises that rely on trusted, governed data but now face a world defined by real‑time decisions, AI‑driven operations, and cloud‑scale innovation. The question leaders are asking has shifted from “Should we modernize BW?” to “How do we evolve BW without disrupting the controls and trust we’ve spent years building?”

SAP’s answer—SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)—signals a fundamental change in how SAP envisions the future of enterprise data. And for BW customers, it represents a rare opportunity: a modernization path that respects the past while unlocking what’s next.

The Pressure on BW Is No Longer Optional

For years, SAP BW has been the backbone of regulated reporting. Its lineage, auditability, and semantic consistency made it indispensable. But the world around it has changed.

The clock is ticking

SAP has extended maintenance for BW 7.5 through 2030 when deployed in Private Cloud Edition, and BW/4HANA remains supported longer. But innovation has clearly moved elsewhere. The runway exists—but it’s finite.

The architecture is showing its age

BW was built for a world where data was centralized, replicated, and processed in batches. Today’s enterprises need something different:

  • Real‑time access to SAP and non‑SAP data
  • Federated architectures that minimize replication
  • AI‑ready data models that can be consumed by Databricks, Snowflake, PowerBI, SAC, and modern analytics tools
  • Elastic cloud scale without the cost of in‑memory sprawl

The gap between what BW was designed for and what enterprises now require is widening.

AI has changed the stakes

AI doesn’t wait for nightly batch loads. It thrives on live, multi‑platform data. And it demands a semantic layer that can be shared across tools—not locked inside a single warehouse.

This is where SAP Business Data Cloud enters the picture.

SAP Business Data Cloud: A New Operating Model for SAP Data

BDC is more than a rebranding of Datasphere. It’s SAP’s vision for a unified, governed, cloud‑native data fabric that brings together:

  • SAP BW (via Private Cloud Edition)
  • SAP Datasphere for semantic modeling and federation
  • SAP Databricks for engineering, ML, and AI(depends on the type of license )
  • SAP Analytics Cloud
  • SAP‑managed data products and insight applications

The most important shift for BW customers is conceptual: BW objects no longer sit at the center. Business data products do.

 

Instead of converting InfoProviders or CompositeProviders, BW becomes a governed source that exposes its logic as reusable data products—consumable across SAP and non‑SAP platforms. This preserves the trust and lineage BW is known for while enabling modern analytics and AI.

The Modernization Patterns Emerging Across the Market

Enterprises aren’t all taking the same path—and SAP isn’t forcing them to. Three patterns have emerged as the most pragmatic and widely adopted.

1. Lift and Shift BW to Private Cloud Edition

A stability‑first approach that buys time without sacrificing compliance.

  • BW moves “as is” to SAP’s managed private cloud
  • No immediate BW/4HANA conversion
  • BW data becomes available as BDC data products

For regulated industries, this is often the first step—a way to modernize the foundation without rewriting anything.

 

 

2. Hybrid Extension with Datasphere and BDC

Datasphere is the most common pattern for organizations that want innovation without disruption to existing system architecture.

In this approach,

  • BW remains the system of record
  • Selected BW objects are exposed as data products
  • New analytics and AI workloads shift to Datasphere, SAC, and Databricks(if needed)

This approach preserves validated BW logic while enabling cloud‑native capabilities where they matter most.

3. Phased Decommissioning Toward SAP BDC

This option is useful for a long‑term transformation for organizations ready to embrace a cloud‑native future with SAP BDC.

  • Business domains are prioritized
  • Semantics are rebuilt in Datasphere
  • BW flows are gradually retired
  • BDC becomes the enterprise data foundation
 

 

It’s the highest‑effort path—but also the one that positions enterprises for AI‑first architectures.

What Changes When BW Evolves into BDC

The shift from BW to BDC is architectural as much as it is conceptual.

Area

SAP BW

SAP Business Data Cloud

Data Model

InfoCubes, ADSOs

Business data products (CDS View Based)

Storage

Centralized HANA

Object store + federation

Integration

ETL‑heavy

Zero‑copy, delta sharing to external systems

(single point of truth in BDC)

Semantics

BW‑specific

Shared semantic layer

AI Readiness

Limited

SAP AI (Joule, Business AI, GenAI)

 

This is a move from a monolithic warehouse to a federated, semantic, AI‑ready data fabric.

The Business Value Emerging from Early Adopters

Organizations modernizing BW through BDC are seeing tangible benefits:

  • Faster delivery of insights through SAP‑managed data products
  • Lower TCO by shifting from in‑memory persistence to object storage
  • AI enablement without exporting sensitive SAP data
  • A future‑proof architecture without a risky big‑bang migration

The value isn’t theoretical—it’s operational.

Why This Matters for Modernization Leaders

For teams already advising on SAP cloud strategy, BDC becomes the umbrella under which modernization efforts converge. It aligns naturally with:

  • Hybrid analytics strategies
  • Cloud‑first data architectures
  • AI enablement roadmaps
  • Phased modernization approaches

It reframes modernization not as a migration project, but as a strategic evolution.

A Natural Next Step for Enterprises

SAP and partners now offer structured pathways—modernization assessments, discovery workshops, and technical enablement—to help organizations map their BW landscapes to a BDC‑aligned future.

The real opportunity is not simply to modernize BW, but to rethink how SAP data participates in the broader enterprise data ecosystem.

What direction are you leaning toward—stability first, hybrid innovation, or a full cloud‑native future?

entry.title: Re: Stop system-wide outgoing email notifications
entry.content:
Dear @vamsikrishnag,

Yes, SAP provides a central supported control for outbound email in test systems via SSCUI 101312 Define Allowed Email Receiver Domains For Email Outbound (allowed receiver domains). This is the standard way to centrally control outbound email delivery from a test tenant by restricting the allowed receiver domains. However, if the goal is to suppress not only delivery but also specific notification types some frameworks such as workflow or situation handling may still need to be adjusted in their own configuration as well

IMPORTANT: Central outbound-delivery control, not a guarantee that every notification mechanism is globally switched off at source. SAP also documents application-specific email controls for example:

  • workflow scenarios where email notifications are configured separately and
  • Situation Handling where email notifications can be disabled for a situation type

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/6214eb663dec486580e16ccdf2ae0220.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/dd2946b00f0546678011fee203cdfd47.html

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3574590

You can also validate the result afterward with the Monitor Email Transmissions app, which SAP documents for monitoring email outbound

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/c5d60e507f584535b74ef5e11ea0c661.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Compatibility Pack: How to check it in your system going forward?
entry.content: In S/4HANA on-premise systems the use rights for compatibility packs (except for LE-TRA / CS / PP-PI) expire at the end of May 2026.  Continuing the usage beyond this date will lead to a breach of contract.  Please contact your SAP representative to discuss you specific situation and how to solve it.

entry.title: Crystal Reports For Visual Studio on AWS
entry.content:
Hi all,

Excuse my lack of knowledge. I've tried pretty hard to find any information on this, so if I missed something, I'm sorry.
I am using Crystal Reports for Visual Studio, and created a IIS website to host a CrystalReportViewer object and render a multitude of reports stored in our DB.
I am struggling with the scenario of a user having long running reports being terminated by AWS's NLB / ALB timeouts. The timeout value I am working with is currently 3600s, or an hour, with the maximum being 4000s.

I am binding the ReportSource of the CrystalReportViewer in Page_Load, and this is giving me a long running HTTP call with no response from the server. Is there any way for us to send keep-alive packets during this synchronous call, or to offload this in the background?
If my thought process is wrong, I would love to know why, and any recommendations you may have.

Thanks,
Ralph

entry.title: Re: Standardizing Material Descriptions (MAKTX) vs. Techn...
entry.content:
Why implement a (strict) convention in an 'unstuctured' Textfield (certainly one with a limited number of 40 CHAR)?
Seems like a 'mission impossible' to get everyone to adhere to conventions with such small nmbr. of characters. (You'll get a bunch of abbreviations that need their own manual, in my estimation ...)
If your company has a UX-Focus on Fiori, using WebGui and Fiori-Apps, I don't even see the value in 'restricting' the proper sequence like "[Product Type] – [Brand] – [Model] – [Specification]" as the 'fuzzy-search' on ProductID or Description in the Browser will show all 'fuzzy' matches.

Personally I would revert to Classification / Characteristics to maintain Structure and allow for decent Reporting, if this is a hard requirement for LoBs like Procurement / Accounting / Finance ...

As a less strict alternative, the Text-Tabs on Product / Material Master ("Basis Data Text, Inspection Text, Internal Comment") can be used to add additional LongText information. These can be queried via table "ESH_SR_LTXT" for Reporting / CDS in higher S/4H-Releases (see SAP-KBA 3048704 - Long text (SAPScript) search enablement for CDS-based search - SAP for Me / Improvement Request Details - Customer Influence) ...

Hope this helps

Nic T.

entry.title: Re: Joule Agent Test fails with an error after adding the...
entry.content:
@Mell 

The error code 6011 (Tenant Administration Worker) usually signals a synchronization or infrastructure timeout within the Joule agent's "Mass Deployment" phase. While your prerequisites seem correct, there are three high-probability causes for this specific failure in Joule Studio when adding an MCP server.

1. The "Additional Property" Mandatory Key

Even if the destination works in Postman, Joule Studio will fail to "mass deploy" the agent if the BTP destination lacks a specific discovery flag.

  • Property Key: sap-joule-studio-mcp-server

  • Value: true

Without this key, the Tenant Administration Worker may fail to authorize the "agent environment" creation, leading to the 6011 error during the job polling.

2. Destination URL Trailing Path Error

A common technical trap in Joule Studio integration is including the protocol path in the BTP Destination URL.

  • The Rule: The BTP Destination URL should NOT end with /mcp.

  • The Reason: Joule Studio automatically appends /mcp to the destination URL when it attempts to communicate with the server. If your destination is https://my-mcp-server.com/mcp, Joule will try to call https://my-mcp-server.com/mcp/mcp, resulting in a 404 or a timeout that triggers the 6011 deployment failure.

Correct Setup:

  • Destination URL: https://your-app-name.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com

  • Joule Studio Path field: /mcp (or your specific base path).

3. Zod Schema vs. Plain JSON Schema

If you are using a custom MCP server (like one built with the FastMCP or standard MCP SDK), Joule Studio requires Zod-based schemas for tool input validation as of late 2025/early 2026.

  • If your MCP tools use plain JSON schema objects for arguments, the "Agent Builder" might fail during the initial metadata handshake (Mass Deploy) because it cannot validate the tool definitions.

  • Solution: Ensure your tool definitions in the MCP server code are using Zod for argument validation.

Summary Checklist for Resolution

CategoryAction
BTP DestinationAdd sap-joule-studio-mcp-server = true to Additional Properties.
URL FormattingEnsure the Destination URL does not include /mcp at the end.
TrustVerify the Joule service instance is in the same subaccount (as you noted).
RolesEnsure your user has the das_developer and extensibility_developer roles.

Note on Service Health: If the above settings are correct, this error can occasionally be a temporary "Integration Gateway" timeout. Since it worked 1 month ago, verify if there was a recent update to your SAP AI Core resource group or if the AI Units quota was shifted, as the "Mass Deploy" requires an active AI Core binding to initialize the agent's LLM reasoning.

SAP Build Joule Studio Agentic Development

This video provides a step-by-step walkthrough of creating and deploying Joule agents with MCP servers, which helps clarify the specific configuration steps where deployment errors often occur.

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
Day 10, an evening session

In today's fast evolving landscape of enterprise software innovation, the integration of advanced AI assistants like Claude Code into SAP development workflows represents a transformative paradigm shift.

Hahaha, just kidding - asked Perplexity to write the most AI-sounding text in the world. IN the WORRLLLD, as Jeremy Clarkson used to say 🤣🤣🤣. Before he got into farming.

So what have I done today, the last day when I shall write a blog post about it. Asked Claude Code to create the simplest possible local freestyle Fiori application: The Flight Explorer from the /DMO/FLIGHT_READONLY Package in A4H that everyone knows. As per usual, I just directed him to the SAP Package, nothing more. Asked him to use the fiori-freestyle Skill that we created together by auditing a previous, more complex Fiori app that was full of bugs. It took him 19 minutes and 12 seconds:

I was pleased to see that he invoked my cute Haiku sub-Agents when needed. 

The app ended up being 97% correct, except that the departure and arrival times were not displayed correctly. Asked him to fix it, which he did successfully:

 

Then I had an idea. What if I copy the entire conversation, give it to back Claude (Cowork this time - just to try something new), and ask him to update the fiori-freestyle Skill with lessons learned from the creation of this app.

That actually worked out quite OK. He added four new paragraphs to the Skill:

I was hoping he would update the Skill file directly (being Cowork and all), but he wasn't able to on account of running in some sort of a sandbox and the Skills folder being read-only and whatnot. Never mind, I replaced the file manually. I'm sure Claude Code would have been able to do it. Cowork is for people who fear the Command Line. Do not fear the Command line.

Being paranoid about the size of Skill files, I asked Claude to evaluate whether it was still an OK size. He concluded that no, it wasn't, and that we should do something about it:

Then he proceeded to move parts of the Skill into so-called Reference Files. Modularisation - I love it!

Aaaaand that's as far as I got. Ran out of credits.

But there you have a Skill self-improvement idea right there: After a successful build (of something for which you already have a Skill), feed the entire accumulated context window back to Claude and ask him to improve the Skill with lessons learned. Instruct him to keep the Skill file as close to max 500 lines as possible - if not, create reference files where practical. And how about making an auto loop out of it?

There is just one problem: This would eat up a boatload of tokens. Which is what happened to me today 😅

Sooo, maybe not such a practical idea after all? Well, it still could be, if instead of dumping the full context window on poor unsuspecting Claude you first asked him to extract the lessons learned, and only gave him those for the Skill update. Or something along those lines, I don't know, I'm confusing myself even as I'm writing this. This is too meta for 10 PM.

 

Having found myself sadly bereft of tokens and not being able to turn to my former best friend Codex (whom I haven't even told about the new SSH tunnel to the VPS where our MCPs now live. That's the sort of a friend I am. Not my finest moment.), I resorted to learning some theory. The thing is, I really liked the idea of using an Obsidian vault as a place to store Skills. I queried Perplexity about the feasibility / practicality of such an idea (didn't want to hurt ChatGPT's feelings asking about Claude stuff). What I got back was really encouraging: Turns out there is this additionalDirectories setting in the Claude Code configuration, which you can point to your Obsidian vault and which will tell it to treat the vault as an extra workspace, plus to read its CLAUDE.md.

How cool is this! I'm DEFINITELY setting this up! And then setting up a separate Obsidian vault for Agents. Do you know what you can do with this stuff?! If not, watch a few YouTube videos on Obsidian + Claude. Thank me later.

 

So that's it, folks. I'm concluding my obsessive writing of blog posts with this 10th and final entry. I'll continue geeking out, experimenting and learning - I just won't be writing about it. Until maybe I have a bombshell. Then I'll write again.

In the meantime, have a good time! And remember: Cancel that Netflix subscriptions and get into AI! 🙂

entry.title: From Binders to Breakthroughs: Why Category Management Is Entering a New Era
entry.content:
From Binders to Breakthroughs: Why Category Management Is Entering a New Era 🚀

Category management has long been a cornerstone of effective procurement—but in today’s volatile, data-rich, and digitally accelerated world, the discipline is being redefined. In a recent podcast conversation, Andy Moe, Director of Solution Advisory at SAP, unpacked what category management really means, why many organizations struggle to mature it, and where the future is headed. For SAP customers and partners, the discussion offered a clear message: category management is no longer optional, static, or purely tactical—it’s becoming a strategic engine for resilience, value, and transformation. 💡

Category Management, Demystified 📘

At its core, category management is about understanding three things: where you’ve been, where you are, and where you’re going—for a defined area of spend. Andy describes it as a long-term, documented strategy that aligns procurement decisions with business objectives. 🧭

Importantly, this isn’t just an academic exercise. A strong category strategy enables procurement leaders to engage business stakeholders with credibility, data, and foresight. Whether the goal is cost reduction, risk mitigation, supplier innovation, or quality improvement, category management provides the narrative and evidence needed to drive alignment—especially when stakeholders may initially resist change. 🤝

For many organizations, however, the concept remains unevenly applied. Some have well-developed strategies; others rely on ad hoc decisions, spreadsheets, or annual planning exercises that quickly become shelfware. This maturity gap is where many SAP customers find themselves today. 📊

Credibility Is the Currency 💼

One of the most memorable metaphors from the conversation was Andy’s infamous “binder”—a physical representation of a well-thought-out category strategy. While the binder itself may feel outdated, the principle behind it remains powerful: credibility wins influence. 📂

A category manager who walks into a meeting with data, supplier insights, risk analysis, and a clear understanding of business priorities is far more likely to persuade stakeholders than someone armed only with a savings target. Category management, at its best, is not about enforcing procurement rules—it’s about finding common ground and advancing shared goals. ✅

For SAP customers, this reinforces the value of digitizing and centralizing category intelligence. When insights live in systems rather than in people’s heads (or physical binders), organizations reduce dependency on individuals and increase consistency, transparency, and trust. 🔐

Why Category Strategies Are So Hard to Build ⏳

If category management is so valuable, why do so many teams struggle to implement it effectively? According to Andy, the biggest barrier is time. Procurement professionals are often trapped in low-value, tactical work—leaving little bandwidth for strategic planning. ⏰

Another challenge is the misconception that category management must be “perfect” from day one. In reality, it’s a layered journey. Organizations don’t need advanced analytics, ESG scoring, or supplier innovation models immediately. The foundation starts with basics: understanding spend, forecasting demand, tracking active projects, and aligning on achievable goals. 🧱

Once that baseline is in place, teams can progressively add sophistication—supplier segmentation, risk analysis, contract visibility, performance metrics, and eventually sustainability and innovation lenses. The key insight for SAP customers: progress beats perfection. 📈

Governance Makes the Strategy Real 🔄

A category plan that’s created once a year and never revisited is little more than a PowerPoint exercise. Strong category management requires governance—regular check-ins, performance reviews, and course corrections. 📅

Andy emphasized the importance of quarterly or mid-year reviews between category managers and leadership. These moments create visibility into what’s working, where assumptions have changed, and how external factors—like tariffs, supply disruptions, or market volatility—are impacting outcomes. 🌍

For CFOs and executive teams, this living feedback loop is critical. It allows organizations to reset expectations early, rebalance levers elsewhere in the business, and maintain credibility with the market. Category management, in this sense, becomes a vital input into enterprise decision-making—not just a procurement function. 🧠

Do You Still Need to Be a Category Expert? 🤔

Historically, category managers were expected to be deep subject-matter experts—especially in direct materials or highly technical commodities. While experience still matters, Andy highlighted a major shift: modern tools, templates, and AI-assisted insights are lowering the barrier to entry. 🧑‍💻

Today, technology can surface market trends, benchmarks, and risk signals far faster than manual analysis ever could. That doesn’t eliminate the need for human judgment—but it does change where expertise is applied. Category managers can spend less time gathering data and more time interpreting it, engaging stakeholders, and shaping strategy. 🧩

Not all categories are created equal, however. Strategic, business-critical categories will always require more hands-on governance, while transactional or low-risk areas are prime candidates for automation. This segmentation mindset is becoming increasingly important as organizations scale category management across both direct and indirect spend. 🎯

The Future: Semi-Autonomous, Not Hands-Off 🤖

Looking ahead, Andy sees category management moving toward a semi-autonomous model. Routine tasks will increasingly be handled by systems, AI, and workflows—but humans will remain firmly “behind the wheel.” 🛞

Large language models and digital assistants can help teams get started faster, generate frameworks, and accelerate analysis. But they are not a substitute for foundational data, clean processes, or governance. Organizations that invest early in digitization and structure will be best positioned to benefit as autonomy increases. ⚙️

For SAP customers and partners, the takeaway is clear: the future will reward those who build strong foundations now. Those who delay risk falling further behind as complexity—and opportunity—continues to grow. 🚦

Final Thoughts: Start Small, Think Big 🌱

Category management isn’t about filling out templates or chasing savings in isolation. It’s about creating clarity in complexity, aligning procurement with business strategy, and building credibility through insight. 🧠

Andy’s advice is refreshingly practical: start small, build momentum, and let the discipline evolve over time. With the right mindset, governance, and technology, category management can shift from a misunderstood concept to a true strategic differentiator. 💪

The question for organizations today isn’t whether to invest in category management—but how quickly they can turn it into a living, breathing capability that delivers value year after year.

APPLE:

https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/the-future-of-procurement/id1764078646?i=1000746690310

SPOTIFY:

https://open.spotify.com/episode/2SI9pCaGVK2AoVSF7BKoCA?si=BdENg3j7T9i4pkRG4RX8GA

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Gurram Dhanunjay Reddy and I am passionate about learning SAP
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community! I'm Gurram Dhanunjaya Reddy, and I'm a student studying enterprise technologies and SAP. I'm curious about how companies handle production, supply chains, and business operations using programs like SAP.

I have been investigating various SAP modules and ideas throughout my studies, including enterprise resource planning systems, business process management, and production planning. I like discovering how technology can boost corporate productivity and address actual business issues.

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as a part of the "Hello SAP Community" initiative.

What is your professional life dream?

My career goal is to work as a proficient SAP consultant or technology specialist who can assist businesses in streamlining their operations. I want to assist businesses in their digital transformation by working with cutting-edge enterprise systems. Being a part of that change would mean a lot to me because I think SAP technologies are crucial to modern businesses.

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

I envision myself working in the technology or enterprise software sector and developing a solid grasp of SAP systems within the next five years. I want to work on practical projects where I can put my technical expertise to use and keep learning about new technologies like artificial intelligence, cloud computing, and data analytics. My objective is to develop my ability to use technology to solve challenging business issues.

What is your hobby?

Developing my technical skills and learning about new technologies are two of my favourite pastimes. I like experimenting with business systems, programming, and problem-solving exercises. I occasionally enjoy reading about technological advancements and how they are influencing various industries.

What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

The importance of perseverance and hard work is among the most significant lessons my parents instilled in me. They consistently inspired me to never give up in the face of difficulties and to never stop learning. This way of thinking keeps me motivated for both my academic work and personal growth.

What do you imagine is the future of technology topics like AI and Cloud ?

Technologies like cloud computing and artificial intelligence, in my opinion, will be crucial to businesses in the future. A lot of businesses are already shifting their systems to the cloud and utilising AI for data analysis and better decision-making. Businesses will become more automated, data-driven, and efficient in the future thanks to these technologies. Professionals who are familiar with these technologies will, in my opinion, have a lot of opportunities.

My Experience with SAP TechEd Bangalore

 

I had the chance to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore 2026 this year, and it was a really worthwhile and thrilling experience for me. SAP experts, developers, students, and tech professionals from various locations came together for the event to exchange ideas and knowledge about the most recent advancements in SAP.

I had the opportunity to attend a number of sessions and presentations during the event where specialists discussed emerging trends and technologies within the SAP ecosystem. Many sessions focused on topics such as SAP S/4HANA, cloud technologies, artificial intelligence, and digital transformation. Seeing how these technologies are assisting businesses in streamlining their operations and increasing productivity was fascinating.

 

And now let’s hear from you!

I would love to hear your thoughts.

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

Feel free to share your answer in the comments and tell us about your goals and dreams.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Delete Parked Document for Supplier Down Pa...
entry.content:
Hello Rohini,

@rohini_dutta21 

The parked supplier down payment cannot be deleted while the workflow instance is active. The workflow must first be cancelled or completed before the parked document can be removed.

 

Scenario / QuestionExplanation, Standard Behavior & SAP Notes
Down Payment Request created in February (Doc Type KA)In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, supplier down payment requests created through workflow-based apps are parked until workflow approval is completed. The final posting step occurs automatically via background job.
Workflow approvals completed in MarchWhen workflow finishes, the system attempts to post the accounting document using the original posting date stored in the parked document (February).
Posting period February closedSince period 002/2026 is closed, the background posting fails with error: Posting period not open. The workflow remains in error status.
Why opening the period in QAS did not resolve itIn workflow-managed documents, the posting attempt may still use the original posting context or cached workflow state. Simply opening the period afterward may not re-trigger the posting automatically.
Can the parked document be deleted?❌ Direct deletion is generally not allowed while the document is part of an active workflow instance. Workflow must be completed or cancelled first.
Standard solution option 1Cancel the workflow instance in the Manage Workflows or Flexible Workflow Administration app (depending on configuration). Once the workflow is terminated, the parked document can be deleted.
Standard solution option 2Use the Manage Supplier Down Payment Requests or Display Parked Documents app to reset or delete the parked document after the workflow is cancelled.
Alternative workaroundIf business wants to keep the document: 1️⃣ Temporarily reopen the posting period 2️⃣ Restart or repost the workflow step 3️⃣ Allow posting to complete 4️⃣ Close the period again.
Important system behaviorIn Public Cloud, workflow-managed financial documents cannot bypass workflow control, meaning manual deletion or posting through backend transactions is not available.
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help Portal → Supplier Down Payment Processing SAP Help → Flexible Workflow for Accounts Payable


If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Issue with Balance Carryforward and Legacy Data Trans...
entry.content:
Hello @efsatug94 ,

In S/4HANA, P&L accounts are not physically reset to zero. Instead:

-balances move to retained earnings account
-reports calculate zero opening balance logically

Therefore some trial balance layouts may still show cumulative movements.

 

Question / SettingExplanation, Recommended Value & SAP Notes
P&L balances appearing in 2026 Trial BalanceIn S/4HANA, P&L accounts are not physically cleared during balance carryforward. Instead, the system calculates opening balances dynamically. If settings related to Legacy Data Transfer (LDT) or fiscal year closing are incomplete, P&L balances may appear cumulative.
Legacy Data Transfer Segment – Transfer StatusAfter completing all migration postings and reconciliation, Transfer Status should be set to “Completed.” This indicates that the legacy migration phase is finished and allows normal financial closing processes to proceed correctly.
Highest Fiscal YearThis field should reflect the latest fiscal year in which postings exist. Since your closing process concerns FY2025 and postings are occurring in 2026, the Highest Fiscal Year should be set to 2026.
Closed Fiscal YearThis value represents the latest fully closed fiscal year for Asset Accounting. Because your system started with LDT in 2025, the Closed Fiscal Year should normally be 2024 (the year prior to migration).
Sequence for Balance CarryforwardProper sequence is critical: 1️⃣ Ensure LDT Transfer Status = Completed 2️⃣ Confirm Closed Fiscal Year = 2024 3️⃣ Confirm Highest Fiscal Year = 2026 4️⃣ Run Balance Carryforward (app equivalent of FAGLGV)
Why P&L accounts are not zero in reportsTrial Balance may display cumulative values depending on reporting variant. In S/4HANA Universal Journal, P&L clearing happens logically via retained earnings, not by clearing individual accounts.
Impact of incorrect LDT settingsIf Transfer Status remains “Ongoing,” the system assumes migration is still active and may block proper year-end processing, including balance carryforward behavior.
Where to check settingsApp: Make Company Code Settings – Asset Accounting Specific
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help Portal → Balance Carryforward in S/4HANA Cloud SAP Help → Legacy Data Transfer in Finance Migration


If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: House Bank Currency Showing Incorrectly During Bank-t...
entry.content:
Hello Rohini,

@rohini_dutta21 

This behavior is generally standard, as the app initially derives the transfer currency from the company code currency, not strictly from the bank account currency.

How Currency Determination Works in the App

Priority logic generally follows:

a. Company Code Currency
b. Transaction Currency entered in the transfer app
c. Bank Account Currency

If different currencies exist, SAP automatically performs exchange rate conversion during posting.

Verify:-

-Verify company code currency (likely GBP).
-Confirm bank account currency = USD in Manage Bank Accounts.
-Ensure the bank GL account currency is not restricted to GBP.
-Try selecting USD manually in the currency field — the system should allow it.

 

Scenario / QuestionExplanation, Root Cause & SAP Notes
Bank account maintained with USD currencyThe currency maintained in Bank Account Management (house bank account) defines the actual posting currency of the bank GL account.
In Make Bank Transfer app the currency defaults to GBPThis field is usually derived from the Company Code Currency, not directly from the bank account currency. If your company code currency is GBP, the transfer amount field will initially default to GBP.
Why system behaves this wayThe Make Bank Transfer app posts accounting documents in company code currency context first, then performs currency translation if the bank account is maintained in a different currency.
What happens when posting is executedIf the receiving or paying bank account is maintained in USD, the system automatically performs currency conversion between company code currency (GBP) and bank account currency (USD) during posting.
When USD should appear insteadIf both: • Paying bank account currency = USD • Receiving bank account currency = USD then the transaction currency should default to USD.
Check configurationVerify the following: ✔ Bank Account Currency in Manage Bank Accounts ✔ Company Code Currency in Define Company Code ✔ Bank GL account currency in Chart of Accounts / G/L master
Important behavior in Public CloudThe Amount Currency field represents transaction currency, which can differ from bank account currency. The app allows multi-currency transfers.
Possible cause in your caseSince the company code IQ04 likely has GBP as company code currency, the system defaults the transfer currency to GBP even though the bank account is maintained in USD.
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help Portal → Bank Account Transfers in S/4HANA Cloud SAP Help → Manage Bank Accounts and House Banks

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Homogeneous DMO and DMOVE2S4
entry.content:
Hi Rajewsari/Boris,

We are planning a conversion and migration from an on-premise ECC on HANA system to S/4HANA 2025 FPS00 in RISE (Hyperscaler – AWS, Migration Method-DMOVE2S4 ).

Once the Development system has been successfully migrated to RISE, we would like to reuse the existing on-premise Development system for emergency production transports until the Production system is converted and migrated to RISE. This interim period is expected to be approximately six months following the Development system migration.

As part of the migration to RISE, we will be using a Migration Server to run the AAS and SUM processes.

Could you please advise on the best approach to handle this scenario? Specifically, we would like to understand whether the on-premise Development system can still be reused after the conversion/migration, or if it would be necessary to rebuild the Development system in order to use it for emergency production transports within the existing production landscape.

Thank you in advance for your guidance.

Regards,

Heinz
